Is Claude Code Auto Memory Hogging Your Memory? A Cleanup & Diagnostics Guide

Opened `~/.claude/projects/` and noticed it keeps growing? The first instinct is usually to blame Auto Memory. Turns out Auto Memory doesn't eat your RAM at all — it actually eats Context Window (MEMORY.md loads only the first 200 lines or 25KB) and disk space. And the real culprit bloating that folder isn't memory files, it's conversation jsonl. This post walks through what Auto Memory actually consumes, the `cleanupPeriodDays` auto-cleanup mechanism, `CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY` for fully disabling transcript writes, and how to use `find` to clean out large files manually.
What Are Claude Code Hooks? Auto-Run Lint, Block rm -rf, Inject Context on SessionStart — A Complete Guide

When you're developing with Claude Code, do you find yourself constantly telling it to run Prettier again? Or worrying that one day it'll accidentally fire off rm -rf? This post walks through how Claude Code Hooks work — when events like PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, SessionStart, Notification, and Stop fire, how to set up matcher and command in settings.json, how to block actions with exit code 2 — and runs through practical hooks: auto-format, blocking rm -rf, reinjecting project context after Compact, Mac desktop notifications, plus prompt / agent hook types and /hooks debugging.
What Are Claude Code's Built-in Tools Doing? Understand How It Picks Tools — Then Write Better Prompts

This post is a quick chat about Claude Code's built-in Tools. If you've used Claude Code, you've probably noticed something — sometimes when you ask it to read a file, it just opens it with Read. Other times when you ask "what components does this project have," it goes off and runs Glob and Grep. And occasionally, it takes a detour through Bash's cat or grep, which is confusing because the built-in tools are right there. Claude actually has its own logic for picking tools — this post walks through what built-in Tools Claude Code has, how it decides which one to use, and how to reverse-engineer its tool usage to fix your prompts.
Is Your Claude Code Context Getting Eaten Alive? AGENTS.md and the Complete Context Management Guide

This post is about Claude Code's Context management problem. A lot of people install a bunch of Skills, Plugins, and MCP Servers, then write a giant CLAUDE.md — and before they've even started working, half their Context is already gone. We'll also cover AGENTS.md (a cross-tool universal standard) and how to make sure your Context goes where it actually matters.
Which Claude Code Files Should You Commit? CLAUDE.md, Settings, Rules, Skills — A Complete Breakdown

After you start using Claude Code, your project ends up with a bunch of new files — CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json, rules, skills, and so on. Which ones should you commit? Which are personal settings that shouldn't be pushed up? This post walks through Claude Code's multi-tier settings architecture and load order, so you understand the line between personal and team.
What Is Claude Code Auto Memory? A Complete Guide to the Auto Memory Feature

Tired of having to re-explain your preferences every time you open a new Claude Code conversation? Claude Code actually has an Auto Memory feature that automatically jots down your corrections and preferences during conversations, so you don't have to repeat yourself next time. This post walks through how Auto Memory works, where it's stored, how it gets loaded, and how to manage it.
Want to Develop With Claude Code? This Guide Takes You From Beginner to Advanced

As a developer, you've probably heard of AI Agent dev tools like Gemini, Codex, Claude Code, and so on. Most developers these days subscribe to a few AI Agent tools to speed up their dev workflow. This post picks one AI Agent tool — Claude Code — and walks through how to use it for development. There may be acceleration techniques and usage concepts here you don't know yet, and this post includes practical examples so you can get up to speed faster.
Use a Custom <spec lang="md"> Block in Vue SFC for Smoother AI Development

Lots of devs pair AI tools (Claude Code, Gemini, Copilot, etc.) with their workflow, and Vue Single File Components (SFC) tend to hit some friction along the way. Here's a different approach — adding a custom <spec lang="md"> block so you can use AI tools in SFC with way less hassle.
